original_title: Providing for a joint session of Congress to receive a message from the President on the state of the Union.
plain_title: Setting Up the President's State of the Union Speech to Congress
plain_summary: This bill officially schedules a joint meeting of the Senate and House of Representatives so the President can deliver the State of the Union address. The State of the Union is a speech where the President updates Congress and the country on how things are going and shares plans for the future. This particular bill set that meeting for February 4, 1997.
key_provisions: Schedules a joint session of Congress on February 4, 1997
Allows the President to deliver the State of the Union message
Brings both the Senate and House of Representatives together in one room
who_it_affects: This bill affects all Americans indirectly, since the State of the Union address is where the President publicly outlines national goals and priorities that can shape future laws and policies.
status_plain_english: The Senate approved this scheduling resolution and sent it over to the House of Representatives for their agreement.

original_title: Providing for an adjournment of the two Houses.
plain_title: Congress Takes a Break in February 1997
plain_summary: This bill officially schedules a vacation break for both chambers of Congress in February 1997. The House of Representatives would take time off from February 13 to February 25, while the Senate would pause from February 13 to February 24. Think of it like a school's winter break, but for lawmakers.
key_provisions: House of Representatives takes a break February 13–25, 1997
Senate takes a break February 13–24, 1997
Both chambers resume normal business after their scheduled return dates
who_it_affects: This bill affects members of Congress and their staff, who would be on a scheduled break, and indirectly affects any citizens or groups waiting on congressional action during that period.
status_plain_english: The Senate passed this and sent word to the House, meaning it was nearly finalized and Congress was set to take its scheduled break.

original_title: Providing for an adjournment of the two Houses.
plain_title: Congress Takes a Break for Memorial Day 1997
plain_summary: This bill officially schedules a vacation break for both the House of Representatives and the Senate in late May 1997. The House planned to pause around May 22-23 and return on June 3, while the Senate would pause around May 22-24 and return on June 2. This is a routine scheduling decision that allows lawmakers to go home to their districts during a holiday period.
key_provisions: House takes a break starting May 22 or 23, 1997
House returns to work on June 3, 1997
Senate takes a break from May 22-24 and returns June 2, 1997
who_it_affects: This bill primarily affects members of Congress and their staff, giving them an official scheduled break around Memorial Day weekend in 1997.
status_plain_english: This bill was fully approved and passed without any objections from Congress.

original_title: Providing for an adjournment of the two Houses.
plain_title: Congress Takes a Summer Break in 1997
plain_summary: This bill officially schedules a summer vacation for both the House of Representatives and the Senate in 1997. The House would take a break starting around August 1-2 and return September 3, while the Senate would pause starting around July 31-August 2 and return September 2. Think of it like a school scheduling its summer break — Congress needs an official vote to decide when members can go home.
key_provisions: House of Representatives recesses from around August 1-2, 1997
Senate recesses from around July 31-August 2, 1997
Both chambers return to work in early September 1997
who_it_affects: This bill primarily affects members of Congress and their staff, who would officially be on recess and not conducting legislative business during this period.
status_plain_english: The Senate passed this bill and sent word to the House of Representatives to let them know.

original_title: To correct technical errors in the enrollment of the bill H.R. 2014.
plain_title: Fixing Typos and Errors in a Tax Law
plain_summary: This bill fixes small technical mistakes, like typos or wording errors, that accidentally ended up in a previously passed tax bill called H.R. 2014. Think of it like correcting a typo on an official document to make sure it says exactly what was intended. It doesn't change the actual rules or policies of the tax law — it just cleans up the paperwork.
key_provisions: Fix technical or clerical errors in H.R. 2014
Ensure the tax bill reads exactly as intended
No changes to the actual tax policies themselves
who_it_affects: This bill mainly affects government officials and lawyers who work with tax law, ensuring the official text of the law is accurate and clear.
status_plain_english: The Senate has taken action on this bill and sent a message back to the House, meaning it is moving through the approval process.

original_title: To correct a technical error in the enrollment of H.R. 2160.
plain_title: Fixing a Paperwork Mistake in a Farm and Food Safety Bill
plain_summary: Sometimes when Congress passes a bill, a small clerical or technical error ends up in the final written document. This bill exists just to correct that kind of mistake in a previously passed law that funds farming programs, rural communities, and the Food and Drug Administration. It doesn't change the actual policies or spending — it just cleans up the paperwork.
key_provisions: Fix a technical error in a previously passed bill
Correct the official written enrollment of H.R. 2160
Ensure the law reads as Congress originally intended
who_it_affects: This mainly affects government agencies like the FDA and Department of Agriculture, ensuring the law funding them is written correctly and can be carried out without confusion.
status_plain_english: The Senate has taken action on this bill and sent a message back to the House, meaning it is moving through the final steps of the legislative process.

original_title: Providing for an adjournment of the two Houses.
plain_title: Congress Takes a Break in October 1997
plain_summary: This bill officially schedules a vacation break for both the House of Representatives and the Senate in October 1997. The House would be off from October 9 to October 21, while the Senate would return slightly earlier, on October 20. These scheduled breaks are a normal part of how Congress manages its calendar throughout the year.
key_provisions: House of Representatives off from October 9 to October 21, 1997
Senate on break from October 9, 10, or 11 until October 20, 1997
Both chambers of Congress officially pause their work during this period
who_it_affects: This bill primarily affects members of Congress and their staff, as it determines when they are officially on a scheduled break from legislative work.
status_plain_english: The Senate has taken action on this bill and sent a message about it to the House of Representatives.

original_title: Providing for a joint session of Congress to receive a message from the President.
plain_title: Setting Up a Special Meeting Between Congress and the President
plain_summary: This bill arranges a special joint meeting where both the Senate and the House of Representatives gather together in one room to hear a speech from the President. Think of it like scheduling an all-hands meeting at a company, except it's the entire U.S. Congress listening to the President speak. This particular meeting was set for January 27, 1998.
key_provisions: Schedule a joint session of Congress on January 27, 1998
Allow the President to deliver a message to all of Congress at once
Bring together both the Senate and House of Representatives in one meeting
who_it_affects: This bill primarily affects members of Congress and the President, as it organizes a formal meeting between them, though the President's message would likely address issues affecting all Americans.
status_plain_english: The Senate approved this and sent the decision over to the House of Representatives to agree on the meeting plan.

original_title: To inform the Senate that a quorum of the House has assembled and of the election of the Speaker and the Clerk.
plain_title: House Notifies Senate It Is Ready and Names Its Leaders
plain_summary: This is a formal message from the House of Representatives to the Senate letting them know that enough members have shown up to officially start doing business. It also announces who was chosen to lead the House — Newt Gingrich as Speaker (the top leader) and Robin H. Carle as Clerk (the official record-keeper). Think of it like a new school year starting and announcing who the class president and secretary are.
key_provisions: Confirms enough House members are present to conduct official business
Announces Newt Gingrich as the newly elected Speaker of the House
Announces Robin H. Carle as the newly elected Clerk of the House
who_it_affects: This primarily affects how Congress operates internally, letting the Senate know the House is officially organized and ready to work alongside them.
status_plain_english: This message was accepted by the Senate without any debate or objection, meaning it was fully approved and finalized.

original_title: Authorizing the Speaker to appoint a committee to notify the President of the assembly of the Congress.
plain_title: Telling the President That Congress Is Ready to Work
plain_summary: This bill allows the Speaker of the House to pick a small group of House members to form a committee. That committee then teams up with a similar group from the Senate to officially inform the President that enough members of Congress have shown up to get started. Think of it like taking attendance and then sending a note to the principal saying class is ready to begin.
key_provisions: Speaker appoints a House committee for this task
House committee joins with a Senate committee
Together they notify the President that Congress has enough members present to do official business
who_it_affects: This bill mainly affects members of Congress and the President, as it sets up the formal process for letting the President know that Congress is officially in session.
status_plain_english: This bill has been fully approved and agreed to without any objections, meaning it passed without needing a formal vote.
